topic: TTP: A Time-Triggered Protocol for Fault-Tolerant Real-Time Systems

entries.objective: Understand the difference between event-triggered and time-triggered architectures
entries.prompt: What is the difference between event-triggered and time-triggered systems?
entries.ai_output_summary: ET systems react to events at runtime causing unpredictable behaviour under load. TT systems use a fixed schedule at design time making behaviour fully deterministic.

actions: Compared against Section 1 of Kopetz 1993 paper

entries.reflection: Helped me confirm my understanding before writing the problem statement section. Verified against the original paper.

entries.objective: Clarify what C-state is and why CRC is calculated over it
entries.prompt: What is C-state in TTP and why is CRC calculated over it?
entries.ai_output_summary: C-state holds mode, time and membership. CRC calculated over C-state means any disagreement between sender and receiver automatically causes frame discard.

actions: Verified against Section 5.1 and Section 4.3 of Kopetz 1993

entries.reflection: AI explanation matched the paper. Used it to confirm my understanding of the CRC trick before writing my summary.

entries.objective: Understand fail-silent assumption and its limitations
entries.prompt: What does fail-silent mean and what are its limitations?
entries.ai_output_summary: Fail-silent means a node outputs correct results or nothing at all. Limitation is it requires hardware support and is an assumption not always true in practice.

actions: Added hardware redundancy detail missing from AI answer

issues: Hardware redundancy requirement not mentioned by AI

entries.reflection: Had to go back to the paper to complete the answer. AI gave a starting point only.
